author: @youcanthandlethetruth6976
text: https://www.nature.com/articles/srep01115.pdf?origin=ppub So by heating and cooling, allowing an annealing process to take place, we can create 'Graphite' or graphene layers, in a microwave. I guess we just blast it for some time, let it cool for a few minutes, then keep blasting it. Maybe put the microwave on a switch timer and bam, you have an automated graphite process :)

author: @youcanthandlethetruth6976
text: https://youtu.be/QijyR2kv98k?t=160 We gotta heat that spoil or coke up!

author: @youcanthandlethetruth6976
text: Once you have your graphite, you can make graphene from it using electrolysis or hydrolysis. Once you have your graphene, it is still layered too thick, we can add a solution and sonicate this to break up the leaflets and mix it into our solution. We can then take this 'Ink' and apply it to anything at this point. Robert used a wire mesh and created a very large surface area for a great capacitor. So with all this information in mind, maybe we now understand Robert's process.

replies.author: @justinw1765

author: @captbill279
text: Have you heard of the KonTiki kiln? What is significant is you have a final 'quench' cycle where you dump water to the center of the cone and the steam does a vigorous scouring action, which, hopefully can yield a higher quality bio char for no extra effort. Just inject water into the bottom of the cone, and you have a type of 'tempering process' which I am betting will yield a better bio char. These kon tiki kiln's are known for producing the highest quality bio char. Imagine a controlled quench where you add a water input valve at the base of the cone. The steam creates a vigorous reaction I am certain will yield positive results. Super neat direction with all your experiments! Keep at it!! Cheers  https://youtu.be/1o0QqePtNM4

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
in manufacturing batteries and super capacitors, what properties of graphene make it so suitable for these applications?
My confusion comes from the macro structure of the inks you produce. I am assuming these inks are made up of relatively small sheets of graphene particles, not a macro sheet of graphene. So how does graphene differ from say graphite or carbon soot? I am still trying to work out how these materials produce superior double layers. it is easy to see how a single macro sheet of graphene could produce superior electrical properties if coated on a metal, but how do  multiple layers of irregular connected nano graphite particles work?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: main factors are surface area and connectivity

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith I really do appreciate you taking the time to reply to my comments. As an electronics designer, with an interest in chemistry, I want to understanding the physics and chemistry of your work, but I really want to use the technology you produce in practical components. That is why I constantly ask for the technical specifications of your devices. I am attempting to assess their suitability for practical applications. The electric bicycle demonstration was very impressive, but without knowing the self discharge rate of the battery it is difficult to determine if this is a practical solution. I am very interested in using this battery technology in uninterruptible power supplies. Can you say how long it will be before manufactured battery components using your technology appear on the market?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: unfortunately mate the channel is all about encouraging people to go out and build it for themselves - most of the stuff is just to demonstrate it can be done then encourage folks to give it a go for themselves and create their own devices.

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith I understand that, however you must agree that any practical device must offer consistent performance time after time. I know it takes a lot of time and effort to fully characterise a component but if you do not publish critical specifications how can others determine if their attempts produce better or worse devices. Energy to weight ration is a very important parameter of any battery, but so is self discharge rate, number of charge/discharge cycles and cost, to name but a few. I am a little surprised that you have not automated the characterisation process, to take the grind out of performing the measurements by hand. Having worked in a design lab, I know that once a concept has been proven, product development is dependent on a process of iteration improvement. In circuit design at least three iteration were required from the prototype, even taking full advantage of software simulation tools. My guess is that far more are needed in the development of high capacity batteries and capacitors, but how can you iterate towards improvement without knowing the critical performance specification of the previous prototypes? if others are to emulate your technology with a view of improving on it, they must know the specification to beat. I would understand if this technical information is company confidential, but I would have thought this would also apply to the production process, which you freely publish. I suggest the difference between science and a hobby is detailed measurement.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are right of course mate - it must be consistent and if you build your own device for sale  that is exactly what you will have to do - good luck with it - as you say it is a long road.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
I've been thinking similar, but hard to control the temp with that.  Some sort of steel container treated with say the graphene ink in a box (could even be cardboard) that is treated on the inside with homemade Starlite approximation and a outer layer of Aluminium foil. Two panes of glass with silicone gasket in between for the top.  Then a few small to medium sized Fresnel lenses pointed at it.  Should easily get up to temps around 500*F to 1000*F in the steel container depending on Fresnel lens types. No Fresnel lens pointed at container--probably can get up to around 300 if insulated well and depending on outer conditions (latitude, time of year, etc). 
  As for controlling temps, I would shoot for hotter than one would use,  but install a vent towards the top with a removable insulated, IR reflective, fitted piece that you can adjust the size of the vent gap. 
  It's a pretty inexpensive and easy project, so worth fooling around with and testing.

author: @handleismyhandle
text: The leftover stuff that comes from a charcoal kiln fired to a couple of thousand degrees is HIGHLY conductive. Turns to silver soup if you grind it in water. Must be almost there. Induction heating under high vacuum is a good idea. I tried using a resistance heater in vacuum, but pulls too many amps and burns up the wire. Using a high voltage plasma is also pretty much a waste of time unless you have a LOT of power to burn. The problem with using a microwave is that I could never find anything with a low enough susceptance to contain a vacuum without getting overly hot. All of that heat represents wasted power.

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text: Also, any thoughts on the new batteries reported to use a glass separator and two sodium metal electrodes? Seems interesting if it is actually true, but I can find practically zero information on it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is - the normal graphetisation process is done at 2000-3000 degrees C

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have no thoughts on other people's work mate - well beyond good for them

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text: It would be cool if true. I learned about it while I was looking into an idea for thermionics. I have the idea that the transmission of electrons could be greatly enhanced through a metal vapor if a strong magnetic field was applied. Might work through a vacuum too. Pretty sure the flow would be a lot more organized and efficient along field lines. Anyway, if I get some elemental sodium made up at some point, I will be trying to work it into a battery too.

author: @flashpointrecycling
text:
Rob + Greg - I recently headed an investigation of 7 different food products to find out what they had in common (in the secret sauce) that was causing me to have symptoms of anaphylaxis.  No one wanted to reveal the contents of their recipe.  I had to find a language we could use to communicate without telling the world what exactly "spices" means on a food label. Turned out to be celery root - a new food allergy for me.

So, now I am scratching my butt trying to navigate the language you use to describe the carbon material you are using.  I have watched ALL of your videos for years.  Some many times.  In the early years you concentrated on processing Graphite (natural) to usable graphene inks and demonstrated several methods.  More recently you have done work with seaweed, hemp and bamboo while referring to the carbon product as "structured carbon" and not graphene but with great results.

In this video, Greg introduced several materials as "Graphite" derived from biomass materials.  Then Robert talked about the graphitisation process but also used the "walks like a duck, talks like a duck" analogy.  This insinuates that while it may not be "graphite", it has properties found in or similar to graphite.

Graphitisation
Definition: The conversion of an organic material in an inert atmosphere at 
temperatures in excess of 1800 deg. C to produce a graphite crystal 
structure.

As found at the Royal Society of Chemistry  - no mention of other chemistry, time or pressure. This can not be done in the average household kitchen or even an electric pottery kiln.


 Without telling me the recipe of your secret sauce can you clarify for me.  Duck or Not a Duck?

replies.author: @flashpointrecycling
replies.text: Gary, humble apologies for screwing up your name.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a fair comment mate - truth i believe it to be graphite but without proper characterisation it is always a dangerous thing for a scientist to claim a thing - hence - really I am hedging my bets lol

author: @borisk6969
text: Its a horrible thought, but at a conference I attended back in the early 90's it was suggested that every disposable nappy/diaper ever made was still around in one form or another. Any process which can both deal with a waste stream and provide a "multiuseful" product has got to be worth developing - keep up the good work Robert and Team FWG

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a horrible thought - cheers mate

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text: I was thinking about the claims made for plastic 'never' decomposing myself today, and decided I don't believe it at all. Anytime I encounter old plastic laying around in the woods, it is crumbling. If it was going to be around in 500 years, there would be an awful lot more of it laying around than there is. I would say 50 years at the most is a more reasonable estimate, and that would be exceptional. There is a ton of energy in plastic, and life is exceptionally good at getting energy out of whatever sources are available. It has to be.

replies.author: @borisk6969
replies.text: Blitherer Masterman I understand were you're coming from, but the main material in a disposable nappy is sodium polyacrylate, even if it's of a variety which is considered biodegradable, I suspect that in a compacted landfill site, devoid of oxygen it's not going to biodegrade in our lifetimes. I suspect that the speakers comments were an over simplification, but waste plastic of all types are a major concern, and it is going to take some serious  innovation to deal with it.

replies.author: @Khwartz
replies.text: As I see it in my town, the polyethylene degrades very rapidly in little peace and then tust in matter on weeks BUT I am not sure that it degrades completely and in between we are breathing plastic dust :(

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text: Is it likely that anything that inert will really be a health hazard to anything? I mean, if it is just sitting there, and nothing eats it other than anaerobic bacteria at a slow pace, it doesn't seem like it will be a problem. Don't get me wrong, I don't like looking at garbage, but if you lift up any tarp or jug or anything else that has been sitting there a long time, you are likely to see hundreds of creatures living under it and enjoying the shelter and moisture it provides. It just seems to me that animals find manmade materials as useful as we do, and a lot of our concerns are human prejudice.

author: @DanielSMatthews
text:
Speaking of process how do you remove the salts from carbon sources such as sea weed which is rich in potassium, iron, calcium, iodine and magnesium? Surely electronically useful graphite needs to be free of random doping or it's electrical properties are unpredictable?

What about the notoriously inconvenient to recycle polystyrene? I read a paper recently that indicated that the char rate during pyrolysis  is temperature dependant. They were trying to minimise it and get a higher yield of aromatic compounds but you could take the process in the other direction and maximise the char to convert as much carbon as possible to graphite.

author: @jfausset
text: Rob, that is so exciting you are exceeding the compressive strength by leaps and bounds. However, I think the more important thing is that you are probably exceeding the tensile strength by more than a factor of two. If you have the ability to test in the tensile break test, please do.  You may have what would be necessary for 3-D concrete printing in housing and industrial applications it has not yet been used for.  Apis Cor (Russia), Win Sun (China), or Andrey Rudenko (US) would probably be interested in what you have.  I am super excited for you and your teams.  If you want a sales representative in the US, let me know.

replies.text:
I'm sorry, I think I confused the question by saying "bigger picture".
Of course, if we can recycle waste, we will likely save energy by not needing the coal burning energy from the power-plant down the street or the diesel tractor farming the field, either of those burning fossil fuels and generating CO2 while making new product.   .....What I am asking about is the process:  Sure, it's expected that you need power to heat and/or microwave, stirring motors, pumping, hauling, etc.   But what about the chemical reactions during the process that have graphite/graphene as your desired output?  Are there side reactions producing CO2 or CO or other hydrocarbons?  I ask because if we want to sell your process as green and environmentally beneficial, these days the buyers are going to ask "What is the amount of CO2 (or other waste gases) coming out of your vent / cooling stack / chimney?"

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i know what you meant - unfortunately i can't tell you the process so you don't know what i know - the process reduces the CO2 emission

replies.text: lol - no mate we are building the offices next door and was I putting up rockwall - me and Steve do most of the work  ourselves - Gary and Tony were here this time though - so things have gone a little quicker - when the offices are built i will do a tour - we are quite proud of the fact that we have literally built this ourselves lol

author: @nostradormouse3583
text:
So let's recap the last month; potentially reviving the coal industry by a process turning coal into graphite, potentially creating an industry creating valuable products from recycled domestic food waste, industry changing improvement is material properties of "unbreakable" plastics, discontinuous improvement in construction materials, an incredibly impressive detergent.

So another average month then.

I'm taking bets. The smart money currently says Bob gets knighted BEFORE taking the title 'Graphere Man', and taking to wear his underpants outside his bulletproof lime-green graphene uni-tard.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - when you look at it that way it has been quite a month lol

replies.text: Hi rob I have looked on line and found nothing about turning rubber into Graphene maybe this will be a first.would it be a simple process don't know would it make good Graphene don't know. But there are mountains of the stuff and it's very cheap per ton.

author: @Karabetter
text:
Since your videos  on the HTC of hemp or bamboo, I remember in most cases the material was ground up before you used it, so as to produce flakes or granules. -good stuff.
Q: Have you ever noticed the larger structure of the starting material surviving the process? In other words, if long fibers of hemp were carbonized, did it turn into usefully conductive  cross linked graphene fibers, 2cm or maybe longer, across its entire length?  Does the structure just become too brittle and fragile to be useful that way?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the structure often survives mate

author: @Allwestconstruction
text:
I think you are already there mate, what does it matter if it is graphite or not, you made waste products into something useful

You have just created a whole new industry

The government has laws on the books now that if your product creates waste (such as plastic bottles), and you can recycle it, then it must be available

text: Rice hulls anyone?

replies.author: @OktoPutsch
replies.text:
Whatever yes, but some organic materials should rather be reinjected into some compost process, giving back to your soils. Coffee grounds, for example, are also an excellent soil base for mushrooms culture. In someway, we could adopt different strategies depending on the distance from agricultural zones to consumers. Let's do it cleverly :)
Tip of the day about mushrooms : Paul Stamets books (interesting talk from him at TED2008 : _"6 ways mushrooms can save the world"_ )

replies.text: fair comment mate - but we are talking about using waste streams that have no other use and are a problem
